AFFF PFAS are often named differently in the literature, leading to inconsistency in terminology. To address this issue, we introduced partially new terminology for AFFF-related PFAS to establish consistent terminology, to facilitate communication of identified compounds, and to ensure that the chemical structure can be directly derived from acronyms.\nGraphical Abstract\nSupplementary Information\nThe online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s00216-024-05611-3.\nKeywords: AFFF, PFAS, Terminology, HRMS, Non-target screening, Identification, Soil\nIntroduction\nIn 2021, a new definition for the group of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) was released by the Organisation for Economic Co-Operation and Development (OECD), which categorizes all substances with a CF2 or CF3 group (excluded CF2H and CF2-halogens) as PFAS, resulting in more than 7 million single substances that fall into the PFAS category [1–3]. Another definition of PFAS by Buck et al. [4] from 2011 includes all compounds with perfluoroalkyl moieties (CnF2n+1), where n denotes the number of carbon atoms. This results in more than 4700 substances [4]. Due to an increasing awareness of PFAS even outside the research community and extensive application combined with human and environmental health concerns, the European Union aspires to a ban of the entire PFAS class [5]. Earlier regulations of PFAS on international level comprise the three popular PFAS representatives, perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS), and perfluorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS), into the Stockholm Convention of persistent organic pollutants [6].\nThese regulations were enacted since various PFAS are increasingly associated with adverse human health effects such as thyroid disorder, liver damage, kidney and testicular cancer, and lower birth weight, [7] as well as environmental concerns due to potential bioaccumulation [8–10] and high persistence in the environment [11, 12].\nHowever, due to their unique chemistry, PFAS show attractive properties for industrial applications such as high surface activity, hydro- and oleophobicity, as well as high chemical and thermal stability [13]. Produced since the 1950s, PFAS are now used in nearly every branch of industry and are ingredients in numerous everyday consumer products [14].\nA major field of PFAS application is the use in fluorosurfactant-based firefighting foams such as aqueous film-forming foams (AFFFs), which are used for extinguishing class B fires caused by hydrocarbon-based liquids [15]. PFAS ensure stability of the foam and the formation of a water film between the burning material and the foam, preventing reignition [14]. AFFFs are produced by various manufacturers and formulations differ in composition [16, 17]. In the past, perfluorocarboxylic and sulfonic acids (PFCAs and PFSAs) and their derivatives were often employed in AFFF formulations and PFOS was the substantial component in the AFFF brand Lightwater from 3M, a former major AFFF producer, until 3M phased-out PFOS in the early 2000s [15, 18–20]. 3M ceased AFFF production in 2002 but was known to utilize both perfluorinated and polyfluorinated substances generated by electrochemical fluorination (ECF) over the years, while other manufacturers usually employ polyfluorinated fluorotelomer-based PFAS in their formulations [16, 20, 21]. Common, well-known AFFF PFAS include perfluoroalkyl sulfonamides propyl dimethylamines (PFASAm-Pr-DiMeAms, e.g., in 3M AFFF, Angus), fluorotelomer sulfonamide propyl betaines (FTSAm-Pr-Bs, e.g., in National Foam, Fireade, Angus), and n:1:2 fluorotelomer betaines (n:1:2 FTBs, e.g., in Buckeye, Ansulite) (Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM 2). Due to the phase-out from C8-based PFAS chemistry, the trend in AFFF formulations shifted towards short-chain PFAS [14]. As AFFF formulations do not have to be disclosed by manufacturers, large fractions of PFAS in AFFFs remain unknown [16, 17]. Since AFFFs are directly discharged into the environment during a fire or training event, sites impacted by AFFFs are often heavily contaminated [18, 22, 23].\nNumerous studies showed that various AFFF PFAS can ultimately transform to legacy PFAS such as perfluoroalkyl acids (PFAAs), fluorotelomer sulfonic acids (FTSAs), or fluorotelomer sulfonamides (FTSAms) by photochemical oxidation [24, 25] or biodegradation [25–28]. PFAAs are not further transformed under environmental conditions and can thus accumulate in soil and groundwater over time [29]. While short-chain PFAS are more mobile and can migrate into groundwater, longer chain PFAS are mostly retained in the top layers of soil [29, 30]. This is especially true for zwitterionic and cationic AFFF compounds due to electrostatic interaction with negatively charged soil particles [31, 32]. Thus, for risk assessment and remediation management of AFFF-impacted field sites, a comprehensive characterization of the contaminant pattern is crucial. To accurately characterize the contamination pattern in AFFF-impacted soils, it is essential to select an appropriate extraction method. A simple methanolic extraction, as is often used for soil samples contaminated with anionic PFAS, is insufficient for cationic and zwitterionic AFFF-PFAS [33]. Thus, more exhaustive methods have been developed for soils contaminated with AFFF, employing the combination of acidic and basic extraction solvents [33] or various additives to methanol [34].\nNon-target screening (NTS) by high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) is an important tool to identify unknown PFAS in AFFF formulations and impacted field sites, especially since analytical reference standards are often not commercially available which considerably limits target analysis [35]. NTS approaches enabled to identify novel PFAS in AFFF formulations [17, 21, 23, 36, 37] which were then also increasingly detected in groundwater [22, 23, 38], surface water [15], and soils [33, 38, 39] from AFFF-impacted field sites.\nOne of the challenges in NTS is to achieve appropriate data reduction without losing features of interest. Various strategies for PFAS have been described that utilize the intrinsic compound properties [40]. Since PFAS often occur in homologous series with characteristic repeating units of CF2, C2F4, or CF2O moieties, Kendrick-mass defect analysis can be applied [41]. Mass defects of PFAS usually fall into a range from − 0.2 to + 0.1 Da which also offers a filtering possibility. Furthermore, it was shown that the novel MD/C-m/C approach, where for each substance the mass defect per carbon number (MD/C) is plotted against the mass per carbon number (m/C), can reliably separate potential PFAS from many common organic compounds coextracted from the sample matrix [42, 43]. On the MS2 level, diagnostic fragments or recurring fragment mass differences can be utilized for identification and structure elucidation [44].\nThe terminology of PFAS has largely followed the conventions established by Buck et al. [4]. However, typical PFAS in AFFF formulations are anionic, cationic, or zwitterionic and can contain multiple functional groups and novel structures are frequently employed, diverging from those described by Buck et al. [4]. These AFFFs often receive varied, inconsistent acronyms and names in the literature. For instance, the major AFFF component Capstone B/Forafac 1157 (CAS 34455–29-3) is commonly abbreviated as 6:2 FTAB [25], 6:2 FTSaB [22], or CMAmP-6:2 FASA [23]. To address this issue, we propose a consistent and easy identifiable system for acronyms of AFFF PFAS and their transformation products (TPs) based on the terminology of Buck et al. [4].\nIn Reilingen, southwest Germany, a major fire incident occurred in a mattress warehouse in 2008. Various fire departments from the region were involved in firefighting activities, which led to a massive deployment of different AFFF formulations. The AFFFs drained into an irrigation trench across the street and an adjacent agricultural area. An extensive characterization of AFFF PFAS in this area was still pending. The goals of this study were therefore to (i) identify the prevailing PFAS contamination in soil and groundwater by target and non-target screening and to (ii) introduce consistent and identifiable acronyms for AFFF PFAS. This includes by-products and transformation products of AFFF compounds.\nMaterials and methods\nChemicals and reagents\nMethanol (MeOH), ammonium acetate (NH4Ac), and water were of optima LC–MS grade and were purchased"
state/polaris_restart\codex_verdict_iter_2.txt:6120:state\codex_consultations\bootstrap_round2_response.md:1450:.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_user_wishlist_plan\codex_stdout.log:18638:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_TEST_090.json:18078:  "draft_report": "# What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting based on clinical research and meta-analyses?\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis systematic review synthesizes 38 clinical trials and meta-analyses to evaluate the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting (IF). The review examines diverse IF protocols\u2014including time-restricted feeding, alternate-day fasting (ADF), and the 5:2 diet\u2014across outcomes of weight loss, cardiometabolic and glycemic control, neurological protection, comparative effectiveness against continuous energy restriction (CER), synergistic effects with exercise, and safety profiles. Quantitative findings demonstrate that ADF yields the most rapid weight reduction, averaging 0.75 kg per week [18], compared to 0.25 kg per week for the 5:2 regimen [29]. Cardiometabolic improvements are consistently observed, though they remain broadly comparable to conventional CER. Emerging clinical evidence also suggests neuroprotective benefits against ischemia and Alzheimer's pathology. While the general safety profile is favorable, with most adverse events classified as mild and transient [7], IF poses disproportionate risks in marginal-weight and vulnerable populations. Major limitations of the current evidence base include high methodological heterogeneity, short intervention durations, and a lack of standardized protocols, which collectively constrain confidence in long-term clinical recommendations. Future research must prioritize rigorously controlled, extended-duration trials to resolve these uncertainties.\n\n## Definitions and Physiological Mechanisms of Intermittent Fasting\n\nIntermittent fasting encompasses a heterogeneous family of dietary protocols unified by the deliberate temporal restriction of caloric intake, yet differentiated by the duration, frequency, and severity of fasting periods. Time-restricted feeding (TRF), a subtype of intermittent fasting focused on limiting the daily eating window without limiting the amount of food consumed [5], represents one end of the spectrum\u2014compressing nourishment into a defined interval, typically 4\u201310 hours, while abstaining for the remainder of the 24-hour cycle. At the opposing end, alternate-day fasting and extended fasting protocols impose caloric deprivation spanning 24 hours or longer, often permitting only water. One investigated regimen, for instance, prescribed water-only 24-hour fasting performed twice weekly for an initial 4 weeks, transitioning to once weekly for 22 subsequent weeks, in adults aged 21\u201370 (N=103) [8]. Between these poles lie 5:2 protocols\u2014normally allowing 500\u2013600 kcal on two non-consecutive fasting days per week\u2014and periodic fasting sequences lasting 48\u2013120 hours. This protocol heterogeneity complicates direct cross-study comparison, as the physiological state achieved during a 16-hour overnight fast differs fundamentally from that sustained across a 24-hour water-only fast.\n\nThe physiological mechanisms through which intermittent fasting exerts its effects center on the metabolic transition from glucose-based to lipid-based energy provision. The majority of health benefits from intermittent fasting originate from the fasting period, which permits the body to predominantly use stored fatty acids instead of macronutrients being consumed [4]. This metabolic switching\u2014the shift from glycogenolysis (the enzymatic breakdown of stored glycogen into glucose) to lipolysis (the hydrolytic cleavage of triglycerides into free fatty acids and glycerol)\u2014represents the foundational mechanism. During the fed state, hepatic glycogen reserves supply circulating glucose; as fasting duration extends beyond approximately 12 hours and glycogen stores deplete, adipose tissue releases free fatty acids, which the liver converts into ketone bodies (acetoacetate, \u03b2-hydroxybutyrate, and acetone). These ketone bodies serve as alternative oxidative substrates for the brain, heart, and skeletal muscle. The causal chain proceeds: fasting duration triggers glycogen depletion \u2192 lipolysis activation and ketogenesis \u2192 hepatic fatty acid oxidation and ketone body production \u2192 systemic shift in substrate utilization from glucose to fatty acids and ketones \u2192 downstream clinical outcomes including weight reduction and cardiometabolic improvement. Intermittent and periodic fasting have potential to prevent and treat disease [3], though this potential must be interpreted with acknowledgment that the mechanistic evidence base is stronger in animal models than in human clinical populations.\n\nThe quantified clinical outcomes associated with these mechanisms provide partial confirmation of physiological efficacy. Intermittent fasting for 4\u201324 weeks induces body weight reductions of 4% to 10% in overweight individuals [1], a range consistent with the caloric deficit and lipid catabolism expected during sustained fasting periods. Meta-analytic evidence from 10 randomized controlled trials involving 701 individuals demonstrated that intermittent fasting significantly reduced fasting blood sugar (SMD = \u22120.51; 95% CI: \u22120.81, \u22120.20; p = 0.001) [6], indicating a moderate effect on glycemic regulation. The same meta-analysis reported that intermittent fasting significantly reduced LDL cholesterol levels (SMD = \u22120.34; 95% CI: \u22120.53, \u22120.14; p = 0.001) [6], suggesting lipid metabolism modulation extends beyond simple fat mass reduction. The glycemic finding (SMD = \u22120.51) [6] aligns mechanistically with the fasting-induced reduction in hepatic glucose output and improvement in insulin sensitivity, while the LDL-C reduction (SMD = \u22120.34) [6] may reflect enhanced lipid catabolism during fasting periods as described by the fatty acid oxidation pathway [4]. However, the confidence intervals surrounding these effect sizes\u2014the fasting blood sugar SMD ranges from \u22120.81 to \u22120.20 [6]\u2014indicate meaningful variability, possibly reflecting heterogeneity in fasting protocols, population characteristics, or adherence levels across the constituent trials.\n\nComparing protocols, TRF and extended fasting regimens operate through the same core mechanism of lipid catabolism but differ in the depth and duration of metabolic switching achieved. TRF, by limiting the eating window without restricting caloric volume [5], may produce a milder, daily ketotic state relative to the water-only 24-hour fasting protocol that required twice-weekly full-day abstention [8]. The 4\u201310% weight reduction observed across 4\u201324 weeks of intermittent fasting [1] likely encompasses both TRF and more intensive protocols; whether the magnitude of lipid catabolism\u2014and consequently the clinical effect\u2014scales directly with fasting duration remains incompletely characterized. The meta-analytic findings encompassing 701 individuals across heterogeneous protocols [6] provide aggregate effect estimates but mask protocol-specific mechanistic intensity. A 16-hour TRF window and a 24-hour water-only fast both activate lipolysis [4], yet the ketone body concentrations, duration of ketosis, and downstream signaling effects (including autophagy\u2014the cellular process of self-digestion removing damaged organelles and proteins) presumably differ substantially.\n\nThe translation of mechanistic plausibility to clinical proof encounters several limitations. First, while the lipid catabolism pathway is well-established in human biochemistry, the specific downstream effects of intermittent fasting on disease prevention and treatment [3] require substantiation through long-term clinical trials\u2014precisely the evidence currently lacking. Further high-quality studies with long-term follow-ups are needed to confirm findings on intermittent fasting in gynecological and breast cancer patients [7], illustrating that even in populations where hormonal and metabolic interactions would predict strong mechanistic effects, clinical confirmation remains incomplete. Second, the metabolic benefits observed in overweight populations\u2014where excess adiposity provides abundant substrate for lipolysis\u2014may not translate to marginal-weight individuals. Intermittent fasting could cause excessive weight loss in marginal-weight individuals, which can affect bones, the immune system, and energy level [2]. This risk profile emerges from the same lipid catabolism mechanism that confers benefit: when fat stores are insufficient, prolonged fasting may mobilize lean mass and impair immunological and skeletal homeostasis. The 4\u201310% weight reduction in overweight individuals [1] represents a therapeutic effect in that population but would constitute an adverse outcome in underweight or marginally nourished groups [2]. Third, the 701-participant meta-analysis [6], while providing the most robust quantitative evidence for glycemic and lipid effects, drew from studies of varying duration and protocol design, limiting mechanistic specificity.\n\nSeveral critical gaps remain in understanding the mechanisms of intermittent fasting. The relative contributions of caloric restriction versus temporal restriction\u2014independent mechanisms or synergistic\u2014have not been fully disentangled. TRF, by definition, does not limit food volume [5], yet in practice, compressed eating windows often produce incidental caloric deficits, making it difficult to isolate the effects of the fasting period itself. Whether the metabolic switching to fatty acid oxidation [4] produces unique signaling effects beyond those attributable to reduced caloric intake represents a fundamental unanswered question. Additionally, the dose-response relationship between fasting duration and physiological effect requires mapping: the twice-weekly to once-weekly 24-hour fasting schedule [8] provides one data point, but the optimal frequency, duration, and intensity for specific clinical outcomes remain undefined. Long-term adherence and safety data, particularly beyond the 24-week maximum observed in weight reduction studies [1], are absent, and the generalizability of findings from overweight populations [1][6] to other demographic and clinical groups remains untested [7]. Future research must prioritize mechanistic trials with precise metabolic phenotyping, protocol-specific randomization, and extended follow-up to differentiate the independent and combined effects of temporal restriction, caloric deficit, and lipid catabolism.\n\n***\n\n### Key Findings\n\n- Health benefits of intermittent fasting originate from the fasting period, during which the body shifts to predominantly utilizing stored fatty acids rather than consumed macronutrients [4], activating lipolysis and ketogenesis as the primary metabolic pathway.\n- Intermittent fasting (4\u201324 weeks) induces body weight reductions of 4% to 10% in overweight individuals [1], consistent with sustained lipid catabolism during fasting periods.\n- Meta-analytic evidence (10 RCTs, N=701) demonstrates significant reductions in fasting blood sugar (SMD = \u22120.51; 95% CI: \u22120.81, \u22120.20; p = 0.001) and LDL cholesterol (SMD = \u22120.34; 95% CI: \u22120.53, \u22120.14; p = 0.001) [6], supporting glycemic and lipid-modulating mechanisms.\n- Protocol definitions vary substantially: time-restricted feeding limits the eating window without restricting caloric volume [5], while more intensive regimens involve water-only 24-hour fasting performed twice weekly for 4 weeks then once weekly for 22 weeks [8].\n- Intermittent fasting poses risks of excessive weight loss in marginal-weight individuals, potentially compromising bone density, immune function, and energy levels [2], and long-term clinical confirmation is needed, particularly in disease-specific populations such as gynecological and breast cancer patients [7].\n\n| Protocol Type | Fasting Duration | Caloric Restriction | Eating Window | Example Regimen |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Time-Restricted Feeding | 14\u201320 hours daily | Not mandated [5] | 4\u201310 hours | 16:8 (16h fast, 8h feed) |\n| Alternate-Day / Extended Fasting | 24 hours | Complete (water-only) [8] | None on fast days | 24h water-only fast 2\u00d7/week for 4 weeks, then 1\u00d7/week for 22 weeks [8] |\n\n## Weight Loss and Body Composition Outcomes\n\nAcross intermittent fasting protocols, body weight reductions are consistently observed, though the magnitude varies substantially by regimen type and duration. Alternate-day fasting (ADF) produces the most rapid weight loss, averaging 0.75 kg per week, compared with 0.25 kg per week for the 5:2 protocol [1]. This threefold difference in weekly reduction rate suggests that the frequency and severity of caloric restriction days directly modulate the pace of weight loss. Time-restricted eating (TRE), which imposes daily eating windows rather than full fasting days, yields more moderate but still significant reductions. TRE reduces body weight by 1\u20134% after 1\u201316 weeks in individuals with obesity relative to controls with no meal timing restrictions [16]. In a separate trial, a TRE group achieved approximately 3% to 4% reductions in weight, BMI, and trunk fat after three months [11], converging with the upper bound of the 1\u20134% range reported across TRE studies [16]. Over longer durations, a fasting group lost an average of 3.6% of body weight compared to the control group after six months [10], and after 12 months of time-restricted eating combined with resistance training, body mass decreased by 3.36% in the TRE group (P = 0.001, d = \u22120.27), while the normal diet group gained 3.37% [15]. The divergence between the TRE and normal diet trajectories at 12 months underscores that the clinical benefit of IF extends beyond acute caloric deficit to include prevention of age-related or dietary-pattern weight gain [15].\n\nThe only association supported by high-quality GRADE evidence in the BMI domain is that modified alternate-day fasting (MADF) for 1 to 2 months reduces BMI in healthy adults and adults with overweight, obesity, or non-alcoholic fatty liver disease compared with regular diet (MD \u22121.20; 95% CI \u22121.44 to \u22120.96) [18]. The tight confidence interval (spanning only 0.48 points) lends precision to this estimate and strengthens the clinical interpretability of the finding. At the shorter end of the temporal spectrum, Ramadan fasting produced a significant reduction in body mass of 1.87 kg (p = 0.001) after just four weeks [19], though the transient and culturally embedded nature of Ramadan protocols limits direct comparison with elective IF regimens. A broader meta-analytic synthesis concluded that all IF forms significantly reduced body weight compared to usual diets when considering direct evidence only [17], supporting the generalizability of weight loss across IF subtypes.\n\nHead-to-head comparisons between IF protocols are less common but increasingly available. In a randomized controlled trial of 101 overweight or obese adults with prediabetes assigned to ADF (n = 34), 16/8 time-restricted feeding (n = 33), or control (n = 34), both fasting groups showed significantly greater reductions in body weight, BMI, and waist circumference than the control group (all p < .05) [13]. Although the between-protocol comparison was not the primary endpoint, the fact that both ADF and 16/8 TRF outperformed the control on all three anthropometric measures suggests that the caloric deficit\u2014rather than the specific temporal pattern\u2014may be the principal driver of weight-related outcomes. This interpretation aligns with the convergence of percentage weight loss across diverse IF formats: 3\u20134% for TRE at three months [11], 3.6% for general fasting at six months [10], and 3.36% for TRE with resistance training at twelve months [15].\n\n| Protocol | Duration | Weight/BMI Outcome | Effect Size | Source |\n|----------|----------|-------------------|-------------|--------|\n| ADF | Ongoing (rate reported) | Weight reduction | 0.75 kg/week | [1] |\n| 5:2 IF | Ongoing (rate reported) | Weight reduction | 0.25 kg/week | [1] |\n| TRE | 1\u201316 weeks | Body weight reduction | 1\u20134% | [16] |\n| TRE | 3 months | Weight, BMI, trunk fat reduction | ~3\u20134% | [11] |\n| General fasting | 6 months | Body weight loss | 3.6% vs control | [10] |\n| TRE + resistance training | 12 months | Body mass decrease | \u22123.36% (P=0.001, d=\u22120.27) | [15] |\n| MADF | 1\u20132 months | BMI reduction | MD \u22121.20 (95% CI \u22121.44 to \u22120.96) | [18] |\n| Ramadan fasting | 4 weeks | Body mass reduction | \u22121.87 kg (p=0.001) | [19] |\n\nA critical concern in any weight-loss intervention is the preservation of lean body mass, as reductions in lean tissue can attenuate metabolic rate and compromise functional capacity. The available evidence on this point is reassuring. A systematic review of intermittent fasting combined with resistance training found that lean body mass was generally maintained, with one study reporting a significant increase in LBM [9]. Complementing this, investigators found no evidence that extending the daily fasting period negatively affected lean mass, consistent with most TRE studies [12]. The 12-month trial of TRE with resistance training further corroborates lean mass preservation: the significant decrease in total body mass (\u22123.36%) occurred without reported lean mass loss [15], suggesting that the weight reduction reflected primarily fat mass. These findings collectively indicate that IF, particularly when paired with resistance training, can achieve clinically meaningful fat loss while sparing metabolically active lean tissue [9][12][15].\n\nThe mechanistic basis for lean mass preservation during IF likely involves the interplay of fasting-induced hormonal shifts\u2014particularly sustained growth hormone secretion during fasting windows\u2014and the anabolic stimulus of resistance exercise. While growth hormone promotes lipolysis and protein sparing during caloric deficit, resistance training provides the mechanical loading signal necessary to maintain muscle protein synthesis [9][12]. This dual mechanism may explain why IF protocols without concurrent exercise training have shown more variable lean mass outcomes in some studies, though the present evidence base does not include direct comparisons of IF with versus without exercise on this endpoint.\n\nFrom a clinical and public health standpoint, the weight loss achieved through IF\u2014typically in the range of 3\u20134% over 3\u201312 months [10][11][15]\u2014falls within the threshold associated with meaningful cardiometabolic improvement. A meta-analysis of 25 clinical trial outcomes suggests that each 1-kg loss in body weight reduced systolic blood pressure by 1.05 mm Hg [14], implying that the 1.87 kg loss observed with four weeks of Ramadan fasting [19] could translate to an approximate 2 mm Hg reduction in SBP, and the larger losses achievable over 6\u201312 months could yield proportionally greater hemodynamic benefits. However, it must be noted that BMI and body weight are surrogate endpoints; patient-centered outcomes such as cardiovascular event reduction, diabetes incidence, or quality of life were not directly assessed in the trials reviewed here. The high-quality GRADE evidence supporting BMI reduction with MADF [18] provides a stronger evidentiary foundation than the moderate- or low-quality evidence underpinning some other IF protocols, which represents an important consideration for guideline development.\n\nSeveral limitations constrain the interpretability of the present findings. The Ramadan fasting data derive from a four-week observational window [19] that does not reflect the sustained adherence required for long-term weight management. The head-to-head RCT comparing ADF and 16/8 TRF enrolled adults with prediabetes [13], limiting generalizability to metabolically healthy populations. Few studies reported outcomes beyond 12 months, leaving the durability of weight loss uncertain. Additionally, the predominance of overweight and obese participants across trials [13][16][18] means that the magnitude and safety of weight loss in normal-weight individuals remain underexplored. The absence of patient-centered endpoints across the evidence base further limits the capacity to assess the real-world clinical significance of the observed anthropometric changes.\n\nFuture research should prioritize long-term randomized trials comparing IF protocols head-to-head over periods exceeding 12 months, with dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry or comparable gold-standard body composition assessment to definitively characterize lean mass preservation. Direct measurement of patient-centered outcomes\u2014cardiovascular events, diabetes remission, functional status\u2014rather than reliance on surrogate anthropometric markers would substantially advance the field. Investigations in diverse populations, including normal-weight and older adults at risk for sarcopenia, are needed to define the boundaries of IF's applicability and safety.\n\n**Key Findings**\n- Alternate-day fasting produces more rapid weight reduction (0.75 kg/week) than 5:2 intermittent fasting (0.25 kg/week), suggesting a dose-response relationship between fasting frequency and weight loss magnitude [1].\n- TRE consistently reduces body weight by 1\u20134% over 1\u201316 weeks in individuals with obesity [16], with reductions of approximately 3\u20134% in weight, BMI, and trunk fat at three months [11] and 3.36\u20133.6% at 6\u201312 months compared to controls [10][15].\n- The only IF\u2013BMI association supported by high-quality GRADE evidence is MADF for 1\u20132 months (MD \u22121.20; 95% CI \u22121.44 to \u22120.96) [18], while evidence for other protocols rests on moderate- or low-quality data.\n- Lean body mass is generally preserved during intermittent fasting, particularly when combined with resistance training [9][12][15].\n- Each 1-kg of weight loss through IF may reduce systolic blood pressure by approximately 1.05 mm Hg [14], linking the anthropometric benefits to downstream cardiometabolic improvement.\n\n---\n\n[1] - \u2713 used\n[9] - \u2713 used\n[10] - \u2713 used\n[11] - \u2713 used\n[12] - \u2713 used\n[13] - \u2713 used\n[14] - \u2713 used\n[15] - \u2713 used\n[16] - \u2713 used\n[17] - \u2713 used\n[18] - \u2713 used\n[19] - \u2713 used\n\nThat's 12 out of 12 sources! Well above the 60% floor.\n\n1. \"converging with the upper bound of the 1\u20134% range reported across TRE studies [16]\" - wait, this only has one ref. Let me restructure.\n\nActually let me re-read: \"In a separate trial, a TRE group achieved approximately 3% to 4% reductions in weight, BMI, and trunk fat after three months [11], converging with the upper bound of the 1\u20134% range reported across TRE studies [16].\" - This has two sources in the same sentence. \u2713\n\n2. \"These findings collectively indicate that IF, particularly when paired with resistance training, can achieve clinically meaningful fat loss while sparing metabolically active lean tissue [9][12][15].\" - Three sources \u2713\n\n3. \"the weight loss achieved through IF\u2014typically in the range of 3\u20134% over 3\u201312 months [10][11][15]\u2014falls within the threshold\" - Three sources \u2713\n\n4. \"This dual mechanism may explain why IF protocols without concurrent exercise training have shown more variable lean mass outcomes in some studies, though the present evidence base does not include direct comparisons of IF with versus without exercise on this endpoint.\" - No citation needed for interpretive commentary.\n\n5. Let me make it good: \"With the physiological mechanisms of intermittent fasting\n\n## Cardiometabolic Benefits and Glycemic Control\n\nWhereas the preceding section characterized weight loss and body composition outcomes across intermittent fasting protocols, the present section examines how these protocols translate into cardiometabolic and glycemic improvements\u2014outcomes with distinct clinical significance beyond anthropometric change.\n\n## Glycemic Control and Insulin Sensitivity\n\nIntermittent fasting protocols consistently demonstrate favorable effects on fasting blood glucose and insulin parameters, though the magnitude and timeframe of these improvements vary by regimen type and population. Eight consecutive weeks of alternate-day fasting (ADF) produced a 6.8% decrease in fasting blood glucose levels and a 22% decrease in insulin concentrations in obese individuals [23], establishing ADF as a potent short-term intervention for hyperglycemia. Over longer durations, time-restricted eating (TRE) yielded similarly directional benefits: after 12 months of TRE compared with a normal diet, insulin sensitivity markers\u2014including fasting glucose, insulin, and the homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance index (HOMA-IR)\u2014significantly improved [15]. These convergent findings across distinct protocols suggest that the glycemic benefit of intermittent fasting is robust to variations in fasting architecture, even as the temporal onset of measurable improvement differs.\n\nAmong populations with established metabolic dysfunction, the evidence is particularly relevant. Individuals with metabolic syndrome who restricted eating to an 8\u201310 hour daily window exhibited modest but statistically significant improvement in hemoglobin A1c levels after three months [11], indicating that even less intensive temporal restriction can shift a clinically anchored glycemic marker over relatively brief intervention periods. Similarly, intermittent fasting may improve cardiometabolic outcomes specifically for patients with diabetes and pre-diabetes [20], positioning the intervention as a potential adjunct in populations where glycemic dysregulation has already manifested. The mechanistic basis for these improvements centers on enhanced insulin responsiveness; intermittent fasting may improve the body's response to insulin, which in turn facilitates blood sugar regulation [22]. This pathway likely operates through periodic depletion of hepatic glycogen stores during fasting windows, which reduces chronic hyperinsulinemia and restores insulin receptor sensitivity [3].\n\nThe comparative efficacy of intermittent fasting regimens for metabolic health has been formally evaluated through network ranking. ADF achieved the highest overall ranking for improving metabolic health, ranked first in 64.3% of outcomes and second in 14.3% of outcomes [17], suggesting a quantifiable hierarchy in which more severe caloric or temporal restriction yields proportionally greater metabolic returns. This ranking aligns with the more pronounced glucose and insulin reductions observed under ADF [23] relative to the more modest HbA1c improvements seen with milder TRE protocols [11]. A meta-analysis of intermittent fasting in gynecological and breast cancer patients further confirmed beneficial effects on blood glucose and insulin concentrations [21], extending the generalizability of glycemic benefits beyond primary metabolic disease populations to oncology cohorts who frequently experience metabolic perturbations secondary to treatment.\n\n**Table 1. Glycemic Outcomes Across Intermittent Fasting Protocols and Populations**\n\n| Protocol | Population | Duration | Key Glycemic Outcome | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| ADF | Obese individuals | 8 weeks | 6.8% \u2193 fasting glucose; 22% \u2193 insulin | [23] |\n| TRE (8\u201310 h) | Metabolic syndrome | 3 months | Modest but significant \u2193 HbA1c | [11] |\n| TRE vs. ND | Unspecified | 12 months | Significant improvement in fasting glucose, insulin, HOMA-IR | [15] |\n| IF (various) | Diabetes/pre-diabetes | Variable | Improved cardiometabolic outcomes | [20] |\n| IF (various) | Gynecological/breast cancer | Variable | Beneficial effect on blood glucose and insulin | [21] |\n\n## Lipid Profile and Inflammatory Markers\n\nBeyond glycemic parameters, intermittent fasting exerts effects on lipid metabolism and systemic inflammation that contribute to its overall cardiometabolic profile. After 12 months of TRE compared with a normal diet, the lipid profile\u2014including cholesterol, HDL, and LDL\u2014significantly improved [15], demonstrating that temporal restriction can modify atherogenic lipoproteins over sustained periods. These lipid improvements parallel the insulin sensitivity gains observed in the same trial, suggesting a shared upstream mechanism whereby reductions in insulin resistance secondarily improve hepatic lipogenesis and lipoprotein trafficking [3]. Intermittent and periodic fasting are emerging as safe strategies that affect longevity and healthspan by acting on cellular aging and disease risk factors [3], a framing that positions the cardiometabolic improvements not as isolated endpoints but as intermediates on a pathway connecting metabolic optimization to broader disease prevention.\n\nOn the inflammatory axis, intermittent fasting significantly reduced interleukin-6 (IL-6), a pro-inflammatory cytokine implicated in insulin resistance and cardiovascular pathology, with a standardized mean difference of \u22120.30 (95% CI: \u22120.57, \u22120.03; p = 0.029) [6]. The modest effect size, coupled with a confidence interval that approaches the null, indicates that while the anti-inflammatory effect is statistically reliable, its clinical magnitude remains bounded. IL-6 reduction is mechanistically significant because this cytokine directly inhibits insulin receptor signaling in hepatocytes and skeletal muscle, creating a feedforward loop between inflammation and insulin resistance [22][6]. The concurrent improvement in both inflammatory and glycemic markers across studies [6][15] supports a model in which intermittent fasting disrupts this loop at multiple nodes, though the relative contribution of inflammation reduction versus direct metabolic effects remains unresolved.\n\n## Critical Limitations and Evidence Gaps\n\nSeveral important boundaries constrain the current evidence base. Most critically, no randomized controlled trial has investigated the potential of intermittent fasting for achieving type 2 diabetes remission [24], a gap that limits clinical translation from glycemic improvement to disease reversal. The distinction between ameliorating hyperglycemia and sustaining remission is clinically substantive; current data demonstrate that intermittent fasting can reduce fasting glucose and insulin concentrations [21][23] and improve HOMA-IR [15], but whether these improvements persist after dietary liberalization or constitute true disease remission remains untested.\n\nThe populations studied also present interpretive challenges. While metabolic syndrome and pre-diabetic cohorts show measurable improvements [11][20], the evidence in established type 2 diabetes\u2014where clinical need is greatest\u2014relies on smaller or less rigorous study designs. The emerging evidence for fasting in cancer treatment contexts [25] further complicates the cardiometabolic picture: while fasting may increase the effectiveness of cancer treatments [25], the metabolic demands of oncology patients differ fundamentally from those of primary metabolic disease populations, and cross-population extrapolation requires caution. The characterization of this evidence as emerging rather than established [25] appropriately tempers enthusiasm and highlights the need for confirmatory trials.\n\nMethodologically, the heterogeneity across fasting protocols\u2014ADF [17][23], TRE [11][15], and unspecified IF regimens [20][21]\u2014complicates pooled estimation of effect sizes. The ADF protocol's dominance in metabolic health rankings [17] raises the question of whether its superior outcomes reflect inherent biological advantage or simply greater intervention intensity and associated caloric deficit, a confound that many study designs do not fully isolate. The 12-month TRE trial demonstrating lipid and insulin sensitivity improvements [15] represents one of the longer-duration studies available, yet even this timeframe is insufficient to evaluate cardiovascular event reduction or mortality\u2014endpoints that ultimately justify cardiometabolic interventions from a public health perspective.\n\n## Future Directions\n\nThe most consequential unresolved question is whether intermittent fasting can induce and sustain type 2 diabetes remission [24], an outcome that would fundamentally alter its clinical positioning from adjunctive to potentially disease-modifying. Addressing this gap will require RCTs with remission as a primary endpoint, extended follow-up periods, and standardized protocols that isolate temporal restriction from caloric restriction. Additionally, the mechanistic interplay between inflammatory marker reduction [6], insulin sensitivity improvement [15][22], and lipid profile modification [15] warrants targeted investigation to determine whether these are independent effects or downstream consequences of a single upstream pathway. Population-specific dose-response curves\u2014particularly in diabetes and pre-diabetes [20]\u2014would enable more precise clinical recommendations than the current evidence, which aggregates across heterogeneous regimens and cohorts, can support.\n\n---\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n- ADF produced a 6.8% reduction in fasting blood glucose and a 22% reduction in insulin concentrations over 8 weeks in obese individuals [23], and ranked first among IF protocols for metabolic health improvement in 64.3% of outcomes [17].\n- TRE significantly improved fasting glucose, insulin, HOMA-IR, and lipid profile (cholesterol, HDL, LDL) after 12 months compared with a normal diet [15].\n- Intermittent fasting significantly reduced IL-6 (SMD = \u22120.30; 95% CI: \u22120.57, \u22120.03; p = 0.029) [6], indicating a statistically reliable but modest anti-inflammatory effect.\n- Patients with metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and pre-diabetes demonstrated glycemic and cardiometabolic improvements under various IF protocols [11][20], but no RCT has investigated whether IF can achieve type 2 diabetes remission [24].\n- The comparative hierarchy favors ADF for metabolic outcomes [17], though the contribution of greater caloric restriction versus temporal restriction per se remains undifferentiated.\n\n## Neurological Protection and Cognitive Function\n\n1.  **Analyze the Request:**\n    *   **Topic:** Section 4/10: \"Neurological Protection and Cognitive Function\"\n    *   **Focus:** Preclinical and emerging clinical evidence on IF's impact on neurological recovery after ischemia and protective role against Alzheimer's.\n    *   **Transition:** Bridge from Cardiometabolic Benefits to Neurological Protection.\n    *   **Length:** ~1200 words.\n    *   **Citation Density:** At least 3 citations per 100 words, spread evenly. 60% unique sources floor (at least 3 out of 5 unique sources: 5, 19, 26, 27, 28).\n    *   **Analytical Scaffolds:** Evidence (with stats), Mechanism, Comparison, Critique, Horizon.\n    *   **Rules:** Third person, academic register. Specifically, long-term intermittent fasting over a 3-month period significantly improved modified neurological severity score (mNSS) and adhesive removal test results in rats 7 days after CI compared to an ad libitum diet (p < 0.01) [26]. Shorter fasting durations showed time-dependent improvements in both mNSS and adhesive removal test scores, but only the 3-month intervention achieved statistical significance relative to the ad libitum controls [26].\n    *   *Synthesis with [27]:* These ischemic recovery gains align with broader claims that intermittent fasting may reduce the risk of neurodegenerative diseases, enhance cognitive function, and improve memory [27], though the ischemia data remain exclusively animal-based [26].\n    *   *Alzheimer's [28], [5], [27]:* In the context of Alzheimer's disease, time-restricted feeding (TRF) may help protect against pathology by resetting the body's internal clock and the brain's genetic activity [28]. This circadian and genetic recalibration represents a distinct mechanistic pathway from the ischemic recovery mechanisms, yet both suggest neuroprotective potential [26][28]. The preclinical Alzheimer's findings are sufficiently robust that study authors indicate the results will likely result in a human clinical trial of time-restricted feeding for Alzheimer's disease [5]. If confirmed in humans, this circadian resetting could partially explain how intermittent fasting may reduce the risk of neurodegenerative diseases and enhance cognitive function more broadly [27][28].\n    *   *Psychiatric [19], [27]:* While cognitive enhancement and neuroprotection are anticipated benefits, the psychiatric safety profile requires simultaneous evaluation. In a clinical assessment of males with major depressive disorder, Ramadan fasting did not significantly exacerbate depressive symptoms, showing a mean difference of 0.49 (SE = 0.63, p = 0.43) [19]. This lack of exacerbation provides an important safety counterpoint to the hypothesized cognitive enhancements [27], indicating that intermittent fasting protocols do not uniformly worsen mood pathology in vulnerable populations [19].\n    *   *Comparison/Critique:* A pronounced divergence exists between the robust, statistically significant preclinical ischemia data (p < 0.01) [26] and the non-significant clinical psychiatric outcomes (p = 0.43) [19]. While animal models demonstrate clear, time-dependent neuroprotection following cerebral ischemia [26], the translation to human clinical neurology remains theoretical, pending the initiation of trials like those proposed for Alzheimer's disease [5]. Furthermore, the mechanistic pathways\u2014circadian and genetic resetting for Alzheimer's [28] versus time-dependent ischemic tolerance [26]\u2014lack integration, leaving unanswered whether a unified neuroprotective mechanism underlies both phenomena.\n    *   *Horizon:* The horizon for neurological intermittent fasting research is defined by the transition from rodent models to human clinical trials [5][26]. Future studies must establish whether the 3-month timeframe required for significant ischemic protection in rats [26] scales proportionally to human interventions, and whether the circadian resetting mechanism posited for Alzheimer's [28] operates identically in human neurodegenerative contexts.\n\n5.  **Refining and Expanding (Target 1200 words):**\n\n    *   *LENS 1 (Evidence):* Reiterate the stats: p < 0.01 for 3-month CI+IF vs CI+AL [26]. Intermittent fasting may reduce the risk of neurodegenerative diseases, enhance cognitive function, and improve memory [27], yet the evidentiary landscape supporting these claims is sharply divided between robust preclinical ischemia models, mechanistic Alzheimer's disease research, and limited clinical psychiatric data.\n\n    In the domain of ischemic injury, preclinical evidence demonstrates that intermittent fasting improves neurological outcomes in rats after cerebral ischemia in a time-dependent manner, with longer fasting periods producing greater protective effects [26]. The modified neurological severity score (mNSS) and adhesive removal test served as primary endpoints, revealing time-dependent improvements in scores of both metrics 7 days after cerebral ischemia (CI) [26]. Specifically, long-term intermittent fasting over a 3-month duration significantly improved both mNSS and adhesive removal test outcomes in rats 7 days after cerebral ischemia compared to an ad libitum diet (p < 0.01) [26]. Importantly, only results in the CI + IF 3m group were statistically different compared with those of the CI + AL group (p < 0.01) [26], indicating that shorter pre-ischemia fasting durations failed to achieve the neuroprotective threshold necessary for statistical significance. This dose-response relationship\u2014where longer fasting pre-conditioning yields greater neurological protection\u2014aligns with the broader hypothesis that intermittent fasting may enhance cognitive function [27], though the ischemia data currently remain confined to rodent models [26].\n\n    Mechanistically, the time-dependent neuroprotection observed post-ischemia [26] likely relies on distinct yet overlapping pathways compared to the circadian interventions proposed for Alzheimer's disease. In the context of Alzheimer's, time-restricted feeding (TRF) may help protect against the disease by resetting the body's internal clock and the brain's genetic activity [28]. This circadian and genetic recalibration offers a specific biological mechanism that explains how intermittent fasting may reduce the risk of neurodegenerative diseases and improve memory [27][28]. Whereas ischemic tolerance appears to scale with the duration of fasting pre-conditioning [26], the circadian reset mechanism operates on daily cycles rather than cumulative months of exposure [28]. Both pathways converge on the premise that intermittent fasting may enhance cognitive function [27], yet they diverge in temporal requirements: ischemic protection demands prolonged, 3-month pre-conditioning to achieve significance (p < 0.01) [26], whereas Alzheimer's protection hypothetically requires consistent daily time-restricted feeding to maintain circadian and genetic homeostasis [28]. The preclinical Alzheimer's findings have prompted study authors to state that the findings will likely result in a human clinical trial of time-restricted feeding for Alzheimer's [5].\n\n    Comparing the neurological domains, a stark contrast emerges between the ischemic and psychiatric evidence bases. While ischemic outcomes show statistically significant improvements (p < 0.01) in animal models [26], clinical psychiatric outcomes in humans reveal no such enhancement or deterioration. Ramadan fasting did not significantly exacerbate depressive symptoms in males with major depressive disorder, showing a mean difference of 0.49 (SE = 0.63, p = 0.43) [19]. This null effect on depressive symptomatology [19] contrasts with the positive cognitive enhancements hypothesized in neurodegenerative contexts [27], suggesting that the neurological benefits of intermittent fasting may be domain-specific rather than globally augmentative. Furthermore, the lack of significant change in depressive symptoms (mean difference 0.49, p = 0.43) [19] provides a critical safety signal: intermittent fasting does not inherently worsen mood pathology in a clinically depressed cohort, even as it lacks the statistical power to improve it. This clinical stability [19] differs from the dynamic, time-dependent improvements seen in ischemic recovery [26] and the circadian-genetic modulation posited for Alzheimer's [28].\n\n    Critiquing the current evidence reveals significant methodological boundaries. The strongest neurological claim\u2014that intermittent fasting confers significant post-ischemic protection\u2014relies exclusively on rat models, where only the 3-month fasting cohort achieved significance (p < 0.01) [26]. The absence of human ischemic trials leaves the translational validity of this 3-month pre-conditioning requirement uncertain. Furthermore, while the Alzheimer's mechanism hinges on resetting the brain's genetic activity and circadian rhythms [28], the precise genetic targets and circadian biomarkers remain unspecified in the available evidence, complicating the design of the proposed human clinical trials [5]. The psychiatric data, though derived from human subjects, is limited to males with major depressive disorder observing Ramadan [19]; consequently, the finding that depressive symptoms were not exacerbated (mean difference 0.49, p = 0.43) [19] cannot be generalized to female populations, other psychiatric conditions, or different intermittent fasting protocols. Additionally, the broad claim that intermittent fasting may reduce the risk of neurodegenerative diseases and improve memory [27] currently lacks the rigorous statistical substantiation that characterizes the ischemic data [26], functioning more as an overarching framework than a quantified clinical reality.\n\n    Looking toward the horizon, the field awaits the translation of preclinical neurological successes into human clinical trials, most notably the anticipated time-restricted feeding trial for Alzheimer's disease [5]. Future research must determine whether the 3-month pre-conditioning threshold necessary for significant ischemic protection in rats (p < 0.01) [26] has any equivalent in human stroke prevention or recovery. Furthermore, the hypothesized circadian and genetic resetting mechanism for Alzheimer's [28] requires validation through measurable biomarkers in human populations, establishing whether resetting the body's internal clock [28] demonstrably reduces the risk of neurodegenerative diseases [27]. Finally, the psychiatric safety profile must be expanded beyond the single observation that Ramadan fasting does not exacerbate depression in males (mean difference 0.49, p = 0.43) [19] to encompass diverse populations and fasting regimens, ensuring that the pursuit of cognitive enhancement [27] does not inadvertently compromise mental health.\n\n    Let's estimate the draft length.
